text: The surgeon performs an anterior dissection on a female patient with a rectosigmoid tumor. After dissecting omental adhesions from the abdominal wall, a medial to lateral dissection exposes the inferior mesenteric artery. Using diathermy and scissors, the artery is carefully separated from the retroperitoneum. The initial peritoneal incision allows the artery to be lifted off the retroperitoneum, and the ureter is identified.

The surgeon enlarges the dissection window using diathermy, revealing the ureter peristalsing below. The dissection progresses superiorly and inferiorly, preparing the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery for ligation. The ureter is clearly identified to prevent injury, and the inferior mesenteric artery is ligated using a ligature device.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral dissection to separate the underside of the left colon from the retroperitoneum. The cut end of the inferior mesenteric artery is visible, and the inferior mesenteric vein is being transected. The left colon is fully mobilized using diathermy dissection, ensuring the correct surgical planes are maintained. A new port is placed in the left iliac fossa to assist with the dissection of the left colon and splenic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the area of the splenic flexure, which has dense adhesions to the spleen requiring considerable dissection for full mobilization. The tail of the pancreas is visible in the center of the screen, surrounded by relatively fatty omentum due to the patient's high BMI.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to free the left side of the rectum behind the uterus and adjacent to the ovary, using a combination of sharp dissection and diathermy. The mesorectal plane is carefully dissected, with nerves preserved by maintaining distance from them. The mesorectal plane is clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon selects a point of rectal transection approximately five centimeters below the tumor, using energy devices to divide the mesorectum with a ligature. The left side of the rectal mesorectum is fully defined and cleared, with minimal estimated blood loss of around fifty milliliters.

The surgeon transects the median sacral artery and encircles the rectum, dividing the surrounding fatty tissue with numerous blood vessels. The rectum is prepared for stapling, free of fat, and positioned between the jaws of an angled linear staple gun, which is then used to divide the rectum.

A left iliac fascia incision is made, and the specimen is removed. The anvil of the staple gun is inserted into the end of the bowel, which is then reintroduced into the abdomen. A circular staple gun is introduced via the anus, and the spike is brought out from the rectal stump to join with the anvil, creating a circular stapled anastomosis. An air leak test is performed, showing no leaks.
The surgeon checks for hemostasis and performs a further washout of the surgical area. The operation is completed, and the surgical wounds are closed.
